It states that “the increased sympathetic activity induced by diethylpropion is a critical mechanism that enhances metabolic rates, further supporting its utility in treating obesity.” To my knowledge, increased sympathetic nervous system (SNS) activity is a symptom of obesity, and effective obesity treatments typically aim to reduce SNS activity to controlled levels in order to lower comorbidities associated with the condition. Explanation B introduces aspects such as hot flushes and osteoporosis, which are distracting since they relate primarily to the therapeutic effects of estrogens in women. Therefore, I prefer A. However, both explanations fail to provide the actual mechanistic pathway, which involves estrogens suppressing gonadotropin (LH and FSH) release via negative feedback on the hypothalamic–pituitary axis. This leads to a reduction in testicular androgen production, lowering circulating testosterone levels. Because prostate cancer growth is driven by androgens, this reduction in testosterone constitutes the key therapeutic mechanism by which estrogens exert their effect in prostate cancer treatment, so none of the explanations is actually good.
